query: association between disrupted immune regulation and preeclampsia

retrieved_docs.title: Immunology at the Maternal-Fetal Interface -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Key processes such as decidualization and placentation regulate immune adaptation, ensuring fetal survival and a healthy pregnancy. Disruptions in immune tolerance at the maternal-fetal interface can lead to complications, including preeclampsia, recurrent pregnancy loss, and infertility. Understanding the immunology of this interface is crucial for identifying the mechanisms that support normal pregnancy, recognizing factors that contribute to these complications, and developing therapeutic strategies to improve maternal and fetal health. This activity examines immunology at the maternal-fetal interface, focusing on the dynamic interactions between maternal and fetal immune systems. This activity emphasizes the importance of collaboration among interprofessional healthcare providers and their crucial role in understanding immunological processes and their impact on pregnancy management and reproductive health.

retrieved_docs.title: Immunology at the Maternal-Fetal Interface -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy presents a vulnerable situation for both the maternal and fetal dyad, putting them at risk for serious complications. Disruption of fetal tolerance may contribute to the immuno-pathophysiology underlying various pregnancy-related complications. Immune disruption at the maternal-fetal interface can help explain the pathophysiology of conditions such as unexplained infertility, recurrent abortion, endometriosis, preeclampsia, and placental abruption.

retrieved_docs.title: Immunology at the Maternal-Fetal Interface -- Clinical Significance -- Preeclampsia
retrieved_docs.content: Preeclamptic pregnant women exhibit elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-8) and reduced anti-inflammatory cytokines (IL-10). Additionally, increased CD4+ T-cell infiltration in the placenta and an overrepresentation of M1 macrophages are commonly observed in preeclamptic pregnancies. [17] Increased inflammatory response to semi-allogeneic fetal antigens leads to impaired spiral artery remodeling, shallow trophoblast invasion, and placental infarction, predisposing to preeclampsia.

retrieved_docs.title: Immunology at the Maternal-Fetal Interface -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Disruptions in trophoblast invasion can lead to complications for both the mother and fetus. Impaired development of the maternal-fetal interface may result in implantation failure, infertility, recurrent miscarriage, or intrauterine growth restriction (IUGR). [11] [12] The trophoblast invasion is superficial in preeclampsia when compared to unaffected pregnancies. [13] In patients with unexplained infertility, endometrial biopsies have revealed significantly fewer NK cells than in fertile individuals. Additionally, infertility and recurrent spontaneous abortions (RSAs) are indirectly correlated with impaired Treg cell function.

retrieved_docs.title: Immunology at the Maternal-Fetal Interface -- Clinical Significance -- Placental Abruption
retrieved_docs.content: Placental abruption is associated with sterile inflammatory changes resulting from inadequate decidual suppressive activity and elevated levels of cytotoxic immune cells in the decidua. Upregulation of pro-inflammatory cytokines and tissue-specific angiogenic factors, such as vascular endothelial growth factor, disrupts vascular integrity, increasing the risk of placental abruption. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Immunology at the Maternal-Fetal Interface -- Clinical Significance -- Recurrent Spontaneous Abortion
retrieved_docs.content: RSA, defined as 3 or more miscarriages, can result from various factors, including anatomical defects and endocrine disorders such as diabetes, hypothyroidism, and polycystic ovarian syndrome. Immune dysfunction at the maternal-fetal interface may also contribute to RSA, as evidenced by the following: Impaired spiral artery remodeling and overexpression of angiogenic growth factors are associated with RSA. Autophagy levels are reduced in trophoblastic villi of RSA patients. RSA patients exhibit increased mature dendritic cells and decreased immature dendritic cells. Decreased M2 macrophage and Treg cell levels are linked with RSA. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Immunology at the Maternal-Fetal Interface -- Function -- Maternal Tolerance
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal tolerance is essential for a successful pregnancy, enabling the pregnant woman to carry her fetus despite the presence of foreign fetal antigens. When maternal tolerance fails, it can lead to complications such as miscarriage and preeclampsia. Modulation of the leukocyte profile at the maternal-fetal interface supports this tolerance. An imbalance, with an excess of decidual NK cells compared to T cells, may result from epigenetic silencing of T-cell chemoattractants in the stromal cells of the decidua. Cytokines such as IL-10 and transforming growth factor-beta (TGF-β) promote the differentiation of existing T cells into Treg cells in the decidua. Apoptosis also contributes to immune privilege. IL-10 and TGF-β, secreted by decidual macrophages, are crucial in preventing fetal rejection and supporting fetal growth throughout pregnancy, up until delivery. [9]

retrieved_docs.content: The placenta uses various defense mechanisms to protect the fetus from most infections, including:

retrieved_docs.title: Preeclampsia -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: The histopathology of preeclampsia involves a complex interplay of maternal and placental factors. Examination of placental tissue from individuals with preeclampsia often reveals distinct features such as maternal vascular malperfusion (MVM) lesions characterized by abnormalities in the maternal blood supply to the placenta. These lesions may manifest as fibrinoid necrosis, atherosis, and increased syncytial knots. Abnormalities in the trophoblasts, such as increased syncytial knot formation and reduced invasion into the maternal spiral arteries, are commonly observed. Inflammatory responses, including infiltration of immune cells and alterations in the placental vasculature, are evident.

retrieved_docs.title: Eclampsia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Additionally, fetal conditions such as trisomy 13 and mirror syndrome (formerly Ballentyne syndrome), as well as complications from multiple gestations, can precipitate preeclampsia, which may progress to eclampsia if not managed effectively. The premature aging of the placenta and breakdowns in maternal-fetal immune tolerance further exacerbate the risk, indicating a complex interplay of genetic, immunological, and environmental factors in the pathogenesis of eclampsia. [2] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Immunology at the Maternal-Fetal Interface -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Interprofessional healthcare providers, including physicians, advanced practitioners, nurses, and pharmacists, must collaborate to closely monitor maternal and fetal health, manage immunological risks, and prevent adverse outcomes. Regular follow-ups and timely interventions are essential for managing complications. Advanced diagnostic skills and the ability to interpret immunological tests are key to identifying conditions such as preeclampsia or recurrent pregnancy loss. An interprofessional approach is crucial for effectively addressing immune-related pregnancy complications. This strategy involves using evidence-based guidelines, incorporating emerging research, and tailoring care plans to each patient's unique needs to ensure optimal maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: Immunology at the Maternal-Fetal Interface -- Function -- Human Leukocyte Antigen-G
retrieved_docs.content: Human leukocyte antigen-G (HLA-G) is expressed by extra-villous trophoblasts and is crucial in embryo implantation. HLA-G promotes vascular permeability and angiogenesis, aiding in the remodeling of the spiral arteries. Additionally, HLA-G stimulates NK cells to release growth factors that support fetal growth. Lower levels of HLA-G have been associated with pregnancy complications such as placental abruption, preeclampsia, and recurrent miscarriages. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Immunology at the Maternal-Fetal Interface -- Function -- T-Helper Type-2 Cytokines
retrieved_docs.content: At the maternal-fetal interface, the maternal immune response shifts toward T-helper type-2 (Th2) and antibody-mediated immunity rather than cell-mediated immunity. Th2 cytokines, including IL-4, IL-5, and IL-10, increase in this region, promoting an anti-inflammatory state that supports the semi-allogenic fetus during pregnancy. An infective or noninfective insult at the placental level, triggering the secretion of pro-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-6, IL-12, and tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α), can lead to placental damage and result in complications such as preterm labor, abortion, or preeclampsia. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Immune Response -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Hemolytic disease of the fetus and the newborn (erythroblastosis fetalis)

retrieved_docs.title: Eclampsia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Other factors contributing to the development of eclampsia include maternal infections and inflammation, such as periodontal disease and urinary tract infections, which have been linked to an increased risk of preeclampsia. Moreover, maternal obesity, gestational diabetes, and metabolic disorders are significant contributors to the development of eclampsia, likely due to the associated systemic inflammation and endothelial dysfunction. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Digoxin Immune Fab -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Clinical studies have also highlighted the clinical utility of digoxin immune Fab in treating pre-eclampsia, an exaggerated inflammatory condition compounded by altered endothelial function. Pre-eclampsia involves widespread activation of platelets and WBCs and subsequent elevated levels of IL-γ, IL-6, and tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α). Digoxin immune Fab can also downregulate TNF-α-induced endothelial surface adhesion molecules (ICAM, VCAM, and E-selectin). DIF also reduces TNF-α-mediated downregulation of Na + /K + pumps. Maternal serum in women with pre-eclampsia also contains elevated endogenous digoxin-like factors (EDLFs), including cardenolide, ouabain, bufadienolide, and marinobufagenin. These factors can inhibit Na + /K + ATPase activity in erythrocytes and the mesenteric artery. Maternal hypertension during pre-eclampsia occurs when reduced Na + /K + ATPase activity contributes to the accumulation of intracellular calcium, leading to systemic vasoconstriction. Therefore, digoxin immune Fab can improve fetoplacental circulation and symptoms of eclampsia/pre-eclampsia by restoring cellular Na + /K + ATPase activity. Digoxin may also protect against TNF-α-mediated endothelial cell dysfunction to offset the exaggerated inflammatory state. [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 245
retrieved_docs.content: COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY is a broad category of maternal health problems that includes both “Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium” and “Hypertension complicating pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium.” The latter, hypertension in pregnancy, is a specific manifestation of a “Disease of circulatory system complicating pregnancy, childbirth and puerperium,” indicating that it is a circulatory disorder that arises or worsens during gestation, delivery, or the postpartum period. Within the group of other maternal conditions, “Spotting complicating pregnancy, unspecified trimester” is listed as a child condition, and it is further classified under “Early, first or unspecified trimester hemorrhage,” linking spotting to early‑trimester bleeding events. These interrelationships highlight that maternal hypertension and early‑trimester spotting are distinct but related complications that fall under the larger umbrella of pregnancy‑related health issues, each requiring specific clinical attention to mitigate risks to both mother and fetus.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 531
retrieved_docs.content: Databases are clinically associated with مقدمات تسمم الحمل (the introductory material on preeclampsia), الْتِهابُ المَشيماءِ والسَّلَى (inflammation of the placenta and membranes, or chorioamnionitis), and تسمم الحمل (preeclampsia). Newborn Screening Terminology includes concepts that are subsets of مقدمات تسمم الحمل and تسمم الحمل, indicating that the terminology used in newborn screening encompasses the introductory aspects of preeclampsia as well as the condition itself. This interconnection underscores the relevance of both databases and newborn screening terminology to the understanding, identification, and clinical management of these obstetric disorders.

retrieved_docs.content: Cervical insufficiency, postpartum sepsis, retained placenta or membranes without hemorrhage, prolonged pregnancy, other previous delivery, unspecified early‑pregnancy bleeding, and malignant tumor of the placenta are all classified as members of the “Complications and risk factors of pregnancy, labor and delivery (excluding miscarriage and stillbirth) (SMQ).” This SMQ category groups together a range of obstetric conditions that represent significant complications or risk factors during pregnancy, labor, and the postpartum period, emphasizing their importance for clinical assessment, monitoring, and management in maternal‑fetal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2116
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium” encompasses a range of adverse conditions that can arise during pregnancy, labor, or the postpartum period, including the specific complication “Hypertension AND/OR vomiting complicating pregnancy childbirth AND/OR puerperium,” which is recognized as a distinct type of obstetric complication. Within this broader group, the subcategory “COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY” is positioned as a child of the overall complications of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium, indicating that it focuses on adverse events that are primarily associated with the gestational phase itself. Together, these entities illustrate the hierarchical organization of obstetric complications, linking general categories to more specific clinical presentations that are important for diagnosis, monitoring, and management in maternal health care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1910
retrieved_docs.content: Databases serve as important clinical resources that are associated with a range of obstetric conditions. They contain relevant information on الْتِهابُ المَشيماءِ والسَّلَى (chorioamnionitis), a serious infection of the fetal membranes that can affect maternal and fetal outcomes. They also provide data on تسمم الحمل (preeclampsia), a hypertensive disorder of pregnancy that poses risks of organ damage and requires careful monitoring. In addition, databases include information on حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ (bicornuate pregnancy, a uterine anomaly that can influence fetal development and delivery planning). Finally, they cover قَيْء الحمل خفيف الوطأة (mild hyperemesis gravidarum), a condition characterized by moderate nausea and vomiting during pregnancy that may require supportive care. By integrating knowledge on these conditions, databases support clinicians in diagnosis, management, and research aimed at improving maternal‑fetal health.
